title: Suez Canal opens
summary: On 17 November 1869 the imperial yacht L'Aigle, carrying the French empress Eugenie, led a procession of forty-five ships from the new harbour of Port Said down the freshly cut waterway of the Suez Isthmus toward the Red Sea. Ten years of forced labour, financial improvisation and technical improvisation under the direction of Ferdinand de Lesseps had joined the Mediterranean to the Indian Ocean and cut some six thousand miles off the sea route from Europe to Asia. Its opening remade world trade, accelerated the European conquest of Africa and set the stage for Egypt's absorption into the British Empire.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The idea of a canal across the Suez isthmus was as old as the pharaohs. Necho II in the seventh century BC and Darius the Persian a century later had connected the Nile to the Red Sea via the Bitter Lakes; Ptolemy II Philadelphus reopened the waterway in the third century BC; the Roman emperor Trajan restored it around AD 100; the Arab conquerors of Egypt cleaned it out one last time in 640; and the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur closed it deliberately in 767 to starve his rivals in Mecca and Medina. Napoleon's savants surveyed the ground during the French occupation of 1798 to 1801 and their engineer Jacques-Marie Le Pere reported, wrongly, that the Red Sea stood some ten metres higher than the Mediterranean and that a lock canal would be required. Successive Egyptian and European proposals were rejected on those cost grounds until, in the 1840s, Linant de Bellefonds and the Franco-British Suez Canal Studies Society demonstrated that the two seas were level.

The project's driving personality was Ferdinand de Lesseps, a former French consul who had served in Cairo and Alexandria in the 1830s and had become a friend of the young Muhammad Sa'id, the fourth son of Muhammad Ali. When Sa'id became khedive in 1854, Lesseps travelled to Cairo, secured on 30 November 1854 a firman granting him a ninety-nine-year concession to build and operate a canal, and in 1858 raised the four hundred thousand shares of the Compagnie universelle du canal maritime de Suez. Two hundred and seven thousand shares were sold in France, more than half through popular subscription; the British market, hostile from the start, refused to touch them; Sa'id took the unsold balance of ninety-six thousand for the Egyptian government. Work began at Port Said on 25 April 1859.

Excavation was originally to be done by the corvee, the labour tax that the Egyptian state extracted from the fellahin of the Nile Delta. At its peak more than sixty thousand peasants were on the isthmus at any one time under conditions of considerable hardship; contemporary estimates and later Egyptian nationalist accounts have varied wildly, from a few thousand to more than a hundred thousand dead, mostly from cholera, dysentery and sunstroke. In 1863 the new khedive Isma'il, under British diplomatic pressure led by Lord Palmerston, banned forced labour on the project. Lesseps was awarded thirty-eight million francs in compensation by the arbitration of Napoleon III and switched to mechanised excavation, importing steam-powered bucket-chain dredgers designed by the French firm of Borel-Lavalley that could shift enormous quantities of sand and clay. The canal itself was a hundred and sixty-four kilometres long as originally cut, running from Port Said on the Mediterranean south through the low-lying Manzala lagoon, then across the sandy waist of the isthmus to Lake Timsah and the new town of Ismailia, then through the Bitter Lakes and on to Suez on the Red Sea. It had no locks. Its original depth was about eight metres and its width some fifty-two metres at the surface and twenty-two metres at the bottom, permitting the passage of vessels drawing up to eight metres.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The inauguration of the Suez Canal at Port Said, 17 November 1869. Illustration by Édouard Riou.
image.credit: Édouard Riou (1833 to 1900), via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The Khedive Isma'il, whose spending on European fashions, opera houses and infrastructure had earned him both admiration and enormous debt, spared nothing for the inauguration. He extended personal invitations to every European sovereign and sent invitations by name to some six thousand dignitaries. The Empress Eugenie of France, cousin of Lesseps, sailed on the imperial yacht L'Aigle; Emperor Franz Joseph of Austria-Hungary came in person, the first European sovereign to visit Egypt since the crusades; the Crown Prince Frederick William of Prussia, the Prince of Wales's younger brother Prince Louis of Hesse, the princes of the Netherlands and Hanover, and ambassadors of Russia, Italy, Spain, the United States and the United Kingdom rounded out the party. In Cairo the khedive built an opera house for the occasion (Verdi was commissioned to write a new work about the pharaohs but did not finish Aida in time, and the theatre opened with Rigoletto instead on 1 November); a new palace, the Gezira, was constructed on an island in the Nile to house the visiting European royalty; and a great tent city on the shore of Lake Timsah at Ismailia was raised to lodge more than a thousand overnight guests.

The formal ceremonies began at Port Said on 16 November 1869. Muslim and Christian clergy blessed the canal from separate platforms erected side by side on the beach; French, British, Italian and Austrian bands played national anthems; fireworks illuminated the harbour into the small hours. On the morning of Wednesday 17 November L'Aigle led the procession into the canal at eight o'clock, followed by the Austrian imperial paddle steamer Greif, the Prussian corvette Grille, the British P&O steamer Delta, the American mail steamer Missouri, the French warship Peluse and forty other vessels of various nationalities. The chief pilot rode on the bridge of L'Aigle; the Empress waved from the rail; the fleet moved through the still-narrow channel at a walking pace, watched from the sandy banks by thousands of Egyptian labourers and their families. Progress was slow, at one point held up by a small Egyptian corvette that had run aground; the flotilla anchored overnight at Ismailia, where the khedive held a state banquet in the tented town for two thousand guests and a fireworks display was staged over the lake. Riou, the illustrator, and the young Ismail Pasha's official photographer recorded the scenes for the commemorative album that was later distributed to the courts of Europe.

On 18 November the flotilla continued south. On 20 November L'Aigle reached the Red Sea at Suez. The Suez Canal was open. On the same day the first commercial convoy of merchant ships was assembled at Port Said to make the passage northward. Within a year some five hundred vessels had transited the canal, most of them British, and the young line of Peninsular and Oriental Steam Navigation had rerouted its India and Australia services from the overland connection at Alexandria and Suez through the new waterway.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The canal transformed the economics of world shipping overnight. The distance from London to Bombay fell from roughly 21,400 kilometres via the Cape of Good Hope to about 11,000 kilometres through the canal; London to Singapore was cut by a third; the tea trade from China accelerated; British India and the East Indies were brought within four weeks of Europe rather than three months. Sail gave way to coal-fired steam because sail was ill suited to the calm waters and steady traffic of a canal transit; the shipbuilding industries of the Clyde, the Tyne and Belfast prospered accordingly. Ports along the new route, Aden, Colombo, Singapore, Hong Kong, Yokohama, boomed. Coaling stations became strategic assets; the great new British base at Aden, taken in 1839, became the essential mid-way stop on the Suez route.

For Egypt the immediate consequences were catastrophic. The canal did not solve Isma'il's fiscal problems; his lavish spending on the ceremonies, the opera house, the Cairo boulevards, the sugar estates in Upper Egypt and the ill-fated conquest of Sudan and Ethiopia drove the state to bankruptcy by 1875. On 25 November of that year, faced with default, Isma'il sold the Egyptian government's forty-four per cent shareholding in the Suez Canal Company to the British government for four million pounds sterling, a deal arranged secretly by prime minister Benjamin Disraeli with a loan from the Rothschilds. Britain thus acquired the largest single interest in the canal and, with it, a direct stake in Egyptian internal politics. Isma'il was deposed by the Ottoman sultan under British and French pressure in 1879; the nationalist rising of Colonel Ahmed Urabi against European interference in 1881 to 1882 gave Britain the pretext to intervene militarily; British troops occupied Egypt after the battle of Tel-el-Kebir on 13 September 1882 and, though nominal Ottoman suzerainty and Egyptian government continued, real power lay with the British consul-general Lord Cromer until 1907. Britain would not leave the Canal Zone until 1956.

The canal also accelerated the wider Scramble for Africa. Suez was too important to Britain to be left near the possessions of any potential rival; the pursuit of the sources of the Nile from Uganda to Ethiopia was driven in part by the strategic logic of the canal. It was equally decisive for the imperial control of Asia. British rule in India, Dutch rule in the East Indies, French rule in Indochina, and later the Japanese victories at Tsushima and Pearl Harbor all became easier to imagine because of the new artery through the isthmus. On the Egyptian side of the ledger, the tens of thousands of fellahin who had died on the works and the loss of the country's own share of the canal to a European power became foundational grievances of Egyptian nationalism. When Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalised the canal on 26 July 1956, provoking the Suez Crisis, he did so from a lectern in Alexandria on the anniversary of the deposition of King Farouk, and in a speech that named Ferdinand de Lesseps forty times as the code word for his intelligence service to seize the canal offices.

sections.heading: Legacy

image.caption: The Suez Canal seen from the International Space Station in 2008, cutting the Sinai peninsula from Egypt proper.
image.credit: NASA / ISS Expedition 16, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The Suez Canal was the largest engineering project of the nineteenth century after the American transcontinental railroad, which had been completed six months earlier at Promontory Summit in Utah. Together they compressed the physical distances of the globe into something like the network of the twentieth century. The Panama Canal that opened in 1914 would complete the transformation, but Suez remained then and remains now the busier and more strategic. Roughly twelve per cent of all global seaborne trade passes through it in a normal year; when the container ship Ever Given ran aground in the canal on 23 March 2021 and blocked it for six days, the shockwave through supply chains reached every continent within a week.

The canal has been enlarged repeatedly. Its depth has been increased from the original eight metres to more than twenty-four; convoy passage was replaced in 2015 by a substantial two-way section built by the Egyptian government of Abdel Fattah el-Sisi at a cost of some eight billion dollars. The Suez Canal Authority, an Egyptian state body, has administered the waterway since the nationalisation of 1956 and derives from it a substantial share of Egyptian foreign exchange earnings. Port Said, Ismailia and Suez have grown into major cities. The Franco-Italian and Greek communities that grew up along the canal in the late nineteenth century, celebrated in the novels of Lawrence Durrell and the memoirs of Andre Aciman, largely dispersed after 1956.

The cultural residue is equally significant. Verdi's Aida, though not premiered at the canal opening as legend has it, was commissioned by Isma'il for the Cairo Opera House and had its first performance there on 24 December 1871; it remains one of the most performed grand operas in the world. The Statue of Liberty was originally sketched by Frederic Auguste Bartholdi in 1869 as a lighthouse for the Mediterranean end of the canal, showing a colossal Egyptian fellaha carrying a torch; Isma'il, out of money, declined the project, and Bartholdi's design was reworked as the Liberty Enlightening the World that would be dedicated in New York Harbour in 1886. The canal, the Statue of Liberty and the transcontinental railroad, all products of the years around 1869, mark the moment when the industrial nineteenth century truly became global.

worldContext.text: The United States on 10 May 1869 had driven the golden spike joining the Union Pacific and Central Pacific railroads at Promontory Summit in Utah, completing the first transcontinental railway. Ulysses S. Grant had been inaugurated as eighteenth president on 4 March; Reconstruction of the defeated South was in its most active phase; the Fifteenth Amendment guaranteeing black male suffrage was ratified in February 1870. In Latin America the disastrous War of the Triple Alliance was still killing Paraguayans; in Cuba the Ten Years' War of independence against Spanish rule had opened in October 1868.

The Meiji government of the emperor of Japan, only in its second year, was in the middle of the Boshin War against Tokugawa loyalists; the fall of the Republic of Ezo at Hakodate on 27 June 1869 completed imperial victory. Compulsory postal service and a national bank were being planned; foreign advisers were arriving in Yokohama by the shipload. The Qing empire under the boy emperor Tongzhi and the regency of the empress dowager Cixi was pursuing the Self-Strengthening Movement, building arsenals at Fuzhou and Shanghai.

British India under viceroy Lord Mayo was expanding its railway and telegraph networks. Cotton exports had boomed during the American Civil War and, though slumping since, remained enormous. The Ilbert Bill controversy over Indian magistrates' jurisdiction over Europeans was still fifteen years off; Indian civil servants were being recruited in tiny numbers. In Afghanistan, Sher Ali Khan had reunified the country after a decade of civil war and pursued a delicate policy between Russian and British spheres.

David Livingstone was somewhere in the region of Lake Tanganyika, out of contact with Europe; Henry Morton Stanley of the New York Herald would sail from New York in October 1869 to find him. In West Africa the Sokoto Caliphate remained dominant; French forces were pushing up the Senegal River. In southern Africa the diamond diggings at Kimberley, opened in the summer of 1869, were drawing prospectors from across the continent and would transform the Cape Colony into a mining economy within a decade.

In Rome the First Vatican Council of the Catholic Church opened on 8 December 1869 and would in July 1870 define the doctrine of papal infallibility. In France the empire of Napoleon III was in its liberal phase, with a new constitution under Emile Ollivier in preparation; the emperor's downfall in the Franco-Prussian War was less than a year off. Britain under Gladstone had disestablished the Church of Ireland in July 1869 and was preparing the Irish Land Act. In Russia, the emancipation of the serfs of 1861 was still being implemented.

New Zealand's Land Wars continued in the Bay of Plenty and East Coast, with the guerrilla leader Te Kooti evading colonial forces after his escape from imprisonment on the Chatham Islands. The Australian colonies were fully self-governing; the eastern colonies were completing their internal railway systems and Melbourne, enriched by the gold rushes, had become one of the largest cities of the British Empire. The Cocos Islands, Fiji and other Pacific groups drifted toward British protection over the following decade.

figures.name: Ferdinand de Lesseps
figures.role: French diplomat and entrepreneur; founder of the Suez Canal Company and director of the project from 1854 to 1869

figures.name: Khedive Isma'il Pasha
figures.role: Ruler of Egypt from 1863 to 1879 who financed the construction and staged the inauguration

figures.name: Empress Eugenie of the French
figures.role: Cousin of Lesseps and guest of honour; her yacht L'Aigle led the inaugural flotilla
figures.credit: Franz Xaver Winterhalter, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Muhammad Sa'id
figures.role: Khedive of Egypt from 1854 to 1863 who granted the original concession to Lesseps

figures.name: Emperor Franz Joseph I
figures.role: Austro-Hungarian emperor who attended the opening in person

figures.name: Linant de Bellefonds
figures.role: French engineer whose surveys established that the Mediterranean and Red Sea were at the same level, making a sea-level canal feasible

images.caption: The inauguration of the Suez Canal at Port Said, 17 November 1869. Illustration by Edouard Riou from the Album of the Empress commemorative volume.
images.credit: Edouard Riou (1833 to 1900), via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Inauguration Ceremony of the Suez Canal at Port-Said, 17 November 1869
